aer_reference: AER-3: Deccan Plateau, hot arid ecoregion | states=['Karnataka', 'Andhra Pradesh (western)'] | rainfall=<740mm | aquifer=crystalline_basement
AER-7: Deccan (Telangana) Plateau and Eastern Ghats, hot semi-arid ecoregion | states=['Andhra Pradesh', 'Telangana', 'Odisha (parts)'] | rainfall=740-960mm | aquifer=crystalline_basement

AER-8: Eastern Ghats, Tamil Nadu Plateau and Deccan (Karnataka), hot semi-arid to arid ecoregion | states=['Tamil Nadu (interior)', 'Karnataka (southern)', 'Andhra Pradesh (eastern)'] | rainfall=500-900mm | aquifer=crystalline_basement

condition.qualitative_description: Block-level SOGE exceeds 90% or CGWB classifies the block as Critical or Over-exploited, indicating extraction is at or beyond the limit of net annual recharge.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: CGWB defines Critical (90–100%) and Over-exploited (>100%) classes based on extraction as a percentage of net annual recharge. In hard rock crystalline basement aquifers of the Deccan Plateau (AER-3, AER-7, AER-8), storativity is extremely low — confined to weathered and fractured zones — so even moderate extraction rates can cause rapid seasonal emptying. A block-level SOGE above 90% in these settings is a strong indicator of chronic stress, though spatial heterogeneity means localised depletion can occur even in nominally 'Semi-critical' blocks.

condition.qualitative_description: The multi-year mean annual groundwater balance (P - ET - Runoff) is negative, indicating chronic depletion where outflows persistently exceed recharge.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: A sustained negative trend in delta_g combined with a negative multi-year mean confirms that groundwater recharge is structurally insufficient relative to extraction and evapotranspiration losses. In semi-arid hard rock settings of peninsular India, even small annual deficits accumulate rapidly due to the low storage capacity of crystalline basement aquifers. This pattern is consistent with the documented acceleration of groundwater level declines in dry regions with extensive croplands globally, and is particularly acute in the Deccan Plateau where monsoon recharge is the sole significant replenishment mechanism.

condition.qualitative_description: Dominant aquifer class is crystalline basement (granite/gneiss) with less than 20% alluvial lithology, confirming low storativity and poor recharge capacity characteristic of Deccan Plateau hard rock terrain.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Hard rock crystalline basement aquifers have very low primary porosity and depend on secondary fractures and weathered zones for storage and transmission. The absence of significant alluvial cover (< 20%) means there is minimal buffering capacity to sustain groundwater levels through dry seasons. In peninsular India, this geological constraint means that over-exploitation manifests as seasonal emptying and filling rather than the gradual long-term decline seen in alluvial aquifers — but repeated seasonal emptying progressively deepens the effective water table and increases pumping costs. This amplifies the impact of any extraction pressure captured by sig_01 and sig_02.

condition.qualitative_description: Fewer than 10 cumulative MGNREGA soil and water conservation works recorded in the micro-watershed, indicating low investment in recharge infrastructure relative to extraction pressure.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: MGNREGA-funded SWC works — including check dams, percolation tanks, farm bunds, and trench structures — are the primary public investment mechanism for enhancing groundwater recharge in semi-arid hard rock terrain. India has a long history of tank and pond construction for managed aquifer recharge, with over 500,000 such structures documented historically. Low cumulative SWC counts indicate that natural recharge opportunities along drainage lines and plateau slopes have not been exploited, amplifying depletion caused by extraction. This signal also identifies an actionable intervention opportunity: MGNREGA-funded recharge infrastructure can reverse depletion trends where governance conditions allow.

condition.qualitative_description: Farmers report that borewell or dug-well depth has increased over recent years, or that existing wells have failed and new deeper borewells were required. In hard rock aquifers, well deepening beyond the primary weathered zone (typically 20–40 m) into fractured basement indicates progressive water table decline.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Well deepening is a direct proxy for declining groundwater table and is particularly diagnostic in hard rock aquifers where the saturated weathered zone is thin. When farmers must drill progressively deeper borewells or abandon shallow dug wells, it confirms that the water table has declined below the level accessible to existing infrastructure. This variable is not available in the framework dataset and must be elicited through farmer interviews.

condition.qualitative_description: Farmers report moderate-to-high borewell/tubewell density (roughly 10–50 or more than 50 within 2–3 km) in hard rock terrain, indicating localised over-extraction not captured by block-level SOGE. Very low density or widespread borewell failure may indicate aquifer limits rather than density-driven stress.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: High local borewell density confirms extraction pressure at micro-watershed scale when paired with landscape depletion signals. Failed or unsuccessful borewells indicate hard-rock aquifer limits and may amplify stress severity without confirming high density per se.

condition.qualitative_description: Farmers report salty/brackish well water, white crust on soil or pipes, or water unsuitable for sensitive crops — indicating salinity from deep penetration or over-extraction.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Reported salinity confirms groundwater stress as a quality-and-quantity problem; may also indicate a distinct salinity pathway requiring solution reassessment.

overall_reasoning_note: Confirm groundwater stress when at least 2 of the primary signals — sig_01 (SOGE > 90% or critical/over-exploited block), sig_02 (Negative mean annual groundwater balance (P−ET−runoff)), sig_05 (Farmer-reported well deepening or dry-season failure) — co-occur. Amplifying signals (do not alone confirm): sig_03 (Dominant hard-rock aquifer (crystalline basement)), sig_04 (Fewer than 10 cumulative MGNREGA SWC works), sig_06 (Farmer-reported high borewell/tubewell density), sig_07 (Farmer-reported saline or brackish well water). prioritise the water balance signal. Always elicit farmer observations on well depth trends and borewell failure rates before finalising diagnosis.

confounders.confounder: Meteorological drought reducing recharge and mimicking over-extraction stress
confounders.how_to_distinguish: Compare mean_annual_precipitation_mm and trend_annual_precipitation_mm against long-term baseline. If precipitation has declined significantly in recent years and soge_dev_percent remains below 70%, drought-driven recharge failure rather than over-extraction is the primary driver. Check whether delta_g decline co-occurs with drought years — if negative delta_g is confined to drought years only, the pathway is drought-induced recharge failure rather than chronic groundwater stress from extraction.

confounders.confounder: Increased cropping intensity or expansion of irrigated area driving extraction rather than aquifer limitation
confounders.how_to_distinguish: Examine trend_cropping_intensity and trend_double_crop_area_ha. If cropping intensity has increased sharply in recent years and SOGE has risen correspondingly, the primary driver is demand-side extraction growth rather than aquifer depletion per se. This distinction matters for solutions: demand management and crop diversification are more appropriate than recharge infrastructure alone when extraction growth is the primary cause.

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Have farmers in this area needed to deepen their borewells or dug wells over the past 5–10 years? Approximately how many metres deeper are new borewells compared to those drilled 10 years ago? Have any existing wells failed completely and been abandoned?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: Confirmed well deepening of more than 3–5 metres over a decade, or widespread borewell failure, strongly confirms the groundwater stress pathway in hard rock terrain where the saturated weathered zone is typically only 15–40 m deep. Rapid deepening (>0.5 m/year) combined with elevated SOGE provides high-confidence diagnosis.

choices.label: Well depth has stayed roughly the same (within ~1 m)
Wells have deepened by more than 2–3 m in the last 5–10 years
Springs or shallow wells have dried up permanently or for much longer

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Roughly how many borewells or tubewells are there in your village or the surrounding 2–3 km area? Has the number of borewells increased significantly in the last 5–10 years?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: High borewell density (more than 50 per village) in a hard rock aquifer with low storativity confirms localised over-extraction that may not be captured by block-level SOGE. Rapid recent growth in borewell numbers indicates accelerating extraction pressure and shifts the diagnosis toward demand-side intervention.

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Have farmers noticed that borewell or well water has become saltier, leaves white or crusty deposits on soil or pipes, or causes crop damage that was not observed previously? Is the water visibly discoloured or does it have an unusual taste or smell?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: Salinity in hard rock aquifers can indicate penetration into deeper saline basement zones as a consequence of over-extraction, or a separate saline intrusion pathway. If salinity is confirmed, the solution set shifts toward water quality management and may require testing before recommending expanded irrigation from existing sources.

choices.label: No — water quality has not changed; no saltiness or deposits
Some salinity or white deposits; still usable for some crops
Brackish or salty water; crop damage or unsuitable for irrigation

sources.title: National Compilation of Dynamic Ground Water Resources of India 2022
sources.relevance_note: Defines SOGE class thresholds (Safe <70%, Semi-critical 70–90%, Critical 90–100%, Over-exploited >100%) used in sig_01; provides block-level groundwater development data for Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, and Odisha.

sources.title: Addressing Depletion in Alluvial Aquifers
sources.relevance_note: Documents the distinct depletion dynamics of hard rock peninsular Indian aquifers — seasonal emptying and filling rather than gradual long-term decline — and argues that management approaches must account for geological differences. Supports sig_03 and the overall reasoning note.

sources.title: Rapid groundwater decline and some cases of recovery in aquifers globally
sources.relevance_note: Global synthesis showing rapid groundwater level declines (>0.5 m/year) are most prevalent in dry regions with extensive croplands, consistent with the semi-arid Deccan Plateau context. Also documents cases of recovery following managed aquifer recharge interventions, supporting the recommended solutions.

sources.title: Sixty years of global progress in managed aquifer recharge
sources.relevance_note: Documents India's extensive historical investment in tanks, percolation ponds, and check dams for managed aquifer recharge in drought-prone semi-arid areas, and the role of CGWB and irrigation departments in artificial recharge. Supports sig_04 and the recommended SWC-based solutions.

sources.title: Taming the anarchy: Groundwater governance in South Asia
sources.relevance_note: Documents hard rock aquifer depletion dynamics in peninsular India and the inadequacy of block-level SOGE to capture localised depletion; supports the overall reasoning note on spatial heterogeneity and the need for participatory groundwater governance solutions.
